metadata.split_role: Expanded Suillus and Leccinum/scaber-stalk bolete records.
metadata.notes: Physical data split only. Species record bodies were preserved; each canonical slug appears in exactly one base mushroom file. Updated in v4.2.82 with 25-record mushroom credibility pass including Black Velvet Bolete and Blue-staining Slippery Jack. v4.2.83: mushroom bolete photo/credibility batch; Commons images added for Neoporphyrellus alboater and Suillus tomentosus; 25-record Suillus/Leccinum/Neoporphyrellus pass. v4.2.85 refreshed Blue-staining Slippery Jack image URLs to direct upload.wikimedia.org files and carried forward v4.2.83 image coverage. v4.2.86: optional photo patches are treated as fill-only for existing base-record image fields; direct image fallback hardened for Black Velvet Bolete and Suillus tomentosus where applicable. v4.2.87-r2026-04-30-mushroom-suillus-leccinum-credibility4: 25-record Suillus/Leccinum credibility pass; source/species-note issues reduced while image/local/taxonomy reviews were retained. v4.2.99-r2026-05-11-fold-mushroom-photo-patches1: folded mushroom photo patch records directly into canonical source records and retired runtime PHOTO_PATCH_PATHS. v4.3.0-r2026-05-11-data-chunk-size-cleanup1 split active data sources by size; record bodies and source order preserved.

review_reason_audit.rule: Normalize explicit review signals into review_reasons/review_note and prefix notes for Needs Review list visibility.

metadata.split_note: v4.3.0-r2026-05-11-data-chunk-size-cleanup1 split this active data source by file size to keep working chunks under roughly 75–100 KB. Record bodies and source order were preserved.

records.culinary_uses: Some Leccinum are eaten, but this group needs thorough cooking and careful host/tree notes. Do not treat scaber-stalk boletes as beginner mushrooms.

mushroom_profile.summary: Orange birch-associated Leccinum with a bright orange cap and darkening scabers; a useful comparison species but still needs cautious ID.
staining: Staining varies by species; slice and observe over 30 minutes
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall; record host tree and staining carefully.
research_notes: Species seed added from the Michigan bolete audit.
Needs image coverage.
Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Look for birch association, orange cap, pale pores, and a scabered stem that darkens with age. Compare carefully with other orange-capped Leccinum.
Source/species-note pass applied in v4.2.87-r2026-04-30-mushroom-suillus-leccinum-credibility4.
pore_color: Whitish to grayish or yellowish pores depending on species
cap_surface: Dry cap; color varies by species

records.edibility_detail: Often listed as edible after thorough cooking, but Leccinum reactions vary and this guide treats it cautiously.
records.field_identification: Look for birch association, orange cap, pale pores, and a scabered stem that darkens with age. Compare carefully with other orange-capped Leccinum.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: The Genus Leccinum
use_links.notes: Scabers, host specificity, pore/staining notes, and North American ID caution.

records.edibility_notes: Some Leccinum are eaten, but this group needs thorough cooking and careful host/tree notes.
curation_notes: Minimal species seed. Added from the Michigan bolete audit for the clean app so the bolete section is more complete; details, seasonality, and images still need a later review.

edible_use.notes: Some Leccinum are eaten, but this group needs thorough cooking and careful host/tree notes.

records.mushroom_card_note: Orange birch-associated Leccinum with a bright orange cap and darkening scabers; a useful comparison species but still needs cautious ID.

records.culinary_uses: Edible by some when young and cooked, but Suillus texture and age matter. Remove old, slimy, or buggy material.

mushroom_profile.summary: Painted Suillus: colorful, shaggy/scaly red to brick cap with yellow pores and eastern white pine association.
staining: May bruise reddish or brownish; flesh can stain pinkish to reddish/brownish
mushroom_profile.season_note: Late summer and fall with eastern white pine.
research_notes: Species seed added from the Michigan bolete audit.
Needs image coverage.
Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Look for eastern white pine, dry shaggy red/brick cap scales, pale yellow pore surface, and a stem with reddish colors. Not the usual slimy Suillus look.
Source/species-note pass applied in v4.2.87-r2026-04-30-mushroom-suillus-leccinum-credibility4.

records.edibility_detail: Edible reputation exists, but texture and personal tolerance vary; peel/clean well and cook thoroughly.
records.field_identification: Look for eastern white pine, dry shaggy red/brick cap scales, pale yellow pore surface, and a stem with reddish colors. Not the usual slimy Suillus look.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Suillus spraguei
use_links.notes: Eastern white pine association, ring, shaggy cap, and staining notes.

records.edibility_notes: Cooked food. Edible by some when young and cooked, but Suillus texture and age matter.

records.notes: Short-stalked Suillus with a very slimy brown cap, yellow pores, no ring, and hard-pine association. Do not rely on shortness alone; host pine and cap/stem details matter.

mushroom_profile.summary: Short-stalked pine Suillus with a slimy brown cap, yellow pores, very subtle glandular dots, and hard-pine association.
host_trees: Hard pines with needles in bundles of 2 or 3

mushroom_profile.summary: Granulated Suillus: sticky orangish-brown cap, yellow non-bruising pores with milky droplets when young, no ring, and glandular dots.

For true S. granulatus, expect 2-needle pine association, sticky orangish-brown cap, small yellow pores often beading milky liquid, no ring, and glandular dots on the stem. North American records need host caution.
Source/species-note pass applied in v4.2.87-r2026-04-30-mushroom-suillus-leccinum-credibility4.
